coredata.dc:title: The novel up/down-conversion dual-emission carbon dots for dual-channel ratiometric fluorescence detection of pH and Cu2+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Yulin
Zhu, Mengqi
Zhou, Qiuju
Shu, Qunwei
Tan, Kejun
coredata.dc:description: Up/down-conversion dual-emission carbon dots (U/D-CDs) are rare and have potential in analytical sensing. Herein, a kind of novel U/D-CDs was prepared successfully by a one-step solvothermal method. The prepared U/D-CDs exhibited similar dual-emission behaviors at excitation wavelengths of 300 nm and 680 nm, respectively. In addition, U/D-CDs displayed good photostability and salt-resistance. Due to the protonation-deprotonation, U/D-CDs showed strong pH dependence in the pH range of 2.0–8.0, which developed an up/down-conversion dual-channel ratiometric fluorescence (FL) probe of pH. The FL intensity of U/D-CDs can be effectively quenched by Cu2+ through the static quenching effect. Meanwhile, an obvious color change from yellow-green to blue can be observed under ultraviolet light with the increase of Cu2+ concentration. The up/down-conversion dual-channel ratiometric fluorescence sensor can be used for the visual sensing of pH and Cu2+, which also eliminates background signals and improves its accuracy and selectivity in complex samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Up/down-conversion
Ratiometric fluorescence
pH response
Copper ions

Up/down-conversion dual-channel ratiometric FL detection of pH
Up/down-conversion dual-channel ratiometric FL detection of Cu2+
The FL quenching mechanism of U/D-CDs induced by Cu2+
Detection of Cu2+ in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HR-TEM (inset) images, (b) Size distribution, (c) XRD spectra, and (d) FT-IR spectra of U/D-CDs.

(a) The UV–Vis absorption and FL emission spectra of U/D-CDs (inset: photographs of U/D-CDs under sunlight and 310 nm UV light). (b) The CIE color coordinates (0.276, 0.434) of U/D-CDs. (c) FL emission spectra of U/D-CDs at different excitation wavelengths (250–350 nm). (d) FL emission spectra of U/D-CDs at excitation wavelengths (640–760 nm).

(a) the FL emission spectra of U/D-CDs with different pH (inset: the photograph of the FL color change of U/D-CDs solution under 310 nm UV light), (b) the relationship between FL intensity ratio (F 544/F 421) and pH value, (c) F 544/F 421 of U/D-CDs at pH = 3.0 and 6.0 after adding 100 μL of various interfering substances (Zn2+, Na+, K+, Al+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cl-, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -, BrO3 -, NO3 –, Cys, Leu, Glu, Arg, and Ala), (d) reversibility of the probe for pH sensing. λex = 300 nm.

At 300 nm excitation wavelength, (a) the FL spectra of U/D-CDs after adding Cu2+ (inset: the photograph of the FL color change of U/D-CDs solution under 310 nm UV light), (b) the relationship between F 544/F 421 and Cu2+ concentration (inset: the linear relationship between F 544/F 421 and Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0–20 μM), (c) the FL responses of U/D-CDs with the addition of 100 μM metal ions. At 680 nm excitation wavelength, (d) the FL spectra of U/D-CDs after adding Cu2+, (e) the relationship between F 544/F 432 and Cu2+ concentration (inset: the linear relationship between F 544/F 432 and Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0–20 μM), (f) the FL responses of U/D-CDs with the addition of different metal ions.

(a) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of U/D-CDs, Cu2+ and U/D-CDs with the addition of 40 μM Cu2+. (b) The UV–Vis absorption spectra of U/D-CDs with the addition of different concentrations of Cu2+. (c) The FL lifetimes at 544 nm of U/D-CDs and U/D-CDs with the addition of 20 μM Cu2+. (d) Zeta potential of U/D-CDs with the addition of different concentrations of Cu2+.
Schematic diagram of the preparation process of U/D-CDs and their application in dual-channel ratiometric FL detection of pH and Cu2+, respectively.

head.ce:title: The novel up/down-conversion dual-emission carbon dots for dual-channel ratiometric fluorescence detection of pH and Cu2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing, 400715, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Guizhou Institute of Technology, Guiyang 550003, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Guizhou Institute of Technology, Guiyang 550003, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Up/down-conversion dual-emission carbon dots (U/D-CDs) are rare and have potential in analytical sensing. Herein, a kind of novel U/D-CDs was prepared successfully by a one-step solvothermal method. The prepared U/D-CDs exhibited similar dual-emission behaviors at excitation wavelengths of 300 nm and 680 nm, respectively. In addition, U/D-CDs displayed good photostability and salt-resistance. Due to the protonation-deprotonation, U/D-CDs showed strong pH dependence in the pH range of 2.0–8.0, which developed an up/down-conversion dual-channel ratiometric fluorescence (FL) probe of pH. The FL intensity of U/D-CDs can be effectively quenched by Cu2+ through the static quenching effect. Meanwhile, an obvious color change from yellow-green to blue can be observed under ultraviolet light with the increase of Cu2+ concentration. The up/down-conversion dual-channel ratiometric fluorescence sensor can be used for the visual sensing of pH and Cu2+, which also eliminates background signals and improves its accuracy and selectivity in complex samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been applied widely in sensing, catalysis, devices, anti-counterfeiting and bioimaging due to their unique fluorescence properties, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and ease of synthesis and surface modification [1–3] . Most CDs reported for analysis applications exhibit single-wavelength emission [4–7]. Ratiometric fluorescence probes based on dual-emission CDs have intrinsic correction capability and can achieve better fluorescence visualization, which has been paid more attention [8,9]. However, most ratiometric fluorescence sensors are based on down-conversion fluorescence, which cannot overcome the limitations of light damage and background fluorescence interference in biosensing [10].

Up-conversion fluorescent materials can convert longer wavelength radiation into shorter wavelength fluorescence, which has many advantages of low damage and high tissue penetration depth [10–12]. The probe based on an up-conversion fluorescent signal can eliminate the background signal interferences and improve its accuracy and selectivity in complex sample analysis. To date, the majority of reported up-conversion fluorescent materials are nanomaterials doped with rare-earth elements and encapsulated organic dyes [13,14]. Only a few up-conversion CDs have been reported. However, few up/down-conversion dual-emission CDs were reported. Therefore, it is of significance to develop up/down-conversion dual-emission CDs.

pH is a key parameter in disease detection, and environmental and food analysis [15,16]. Abnormal pH values may affect physical functions and lead to a variety of serious diseases, such as cardiovascular diseases, cancers and anemia [17,18]. Therefore, developing pH sensors is crucial. Meanwhile, Cu2+ plays an important role in various biological processes. However, excessive Cu2+ may lead to many serious diseases, such as Wilson's disease and Alzheimer's disease [19–21]. Many methods, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy [22] and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [23], have been developed for the detection of Cu2+. But they suffer expensive equipment and complex operations [24,25]. Fluorescence analysis plays an increasingly important role in the detection of metal ions due to its fast response, high sensitivity and selectivity [26–28]. Therefore, it is desirable to develop simpler and more reliable fluorescence sensors to detect Cu2+.

In this work, up/down-conversion dual-emission carbon dots (U/D-CDs) were prepared by a one-step solvothermal method (Scheme 1 ). The prepared CDs exhibited similar dual-emission behavior when the excitation wavelengths were 300 nm and 680 nm respectively. A novel up/down-conversion dual-channel ratiometric fluorescence (FL) sensor based on U/D-CDs has been successfully developed to achieve accurate and selective detection of pH and Cu2+. In this strategy, the emission peak of U/D-CDs at 544 nm exhibited strong pH-dependence with a response range of 2.0–8.0, which achieved the visualization of FL color changes (from blue to yellow-green). In addition, Cu2+ can significantly quench the FL of U/D-CDs through strong static quenching. The up/down-conversion dual-channel ratiometric FL sensor based on U/D-CDs can improve the accuracy and reliability of pH and Cu2+ detection in complex samples.

O-Phenylenediamine (OPD) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Eosin Y (EY) and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O were purchased from Maclean's Biochemical Co., Ltd. Citric acid (CA), NaOH, NaCl, KCl, Mg(NO3)2, ZnSO4, BaCl2, Pb(NO3)2 and other metal salts were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. Anhydrous ethanol was obtained from Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Co., Ltd. All chemical reagents are analytical pure and applied without any further purification.

The FL spectra and UV visible absorption spectra were recorded by the F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer and Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer, respectively. TEM images of U/D-CDs were obtained by Thermo Talos F200X Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy of U/D-CDs were recorded using a thermoelectric Thermo escalab 250XI X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) and a Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR), respectively. The measurement of FL lifetime was conducted on the Edinburgh FLS100 photoluminescence spectrometer.

U/D-CDs were prepared by a one-step solvothermal method. Briefly, 20 mg eosin Y, 60 mg O-Phenylenediamine and 60 mg citric acid were mixed in 5 mL ethanol. After ultrasonic treatment for 5 min, the mixture was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 6 h. When the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, a brown suspension containing U/D-CDs was obtained. The obtained solution was first diluted 10 times using ultrapure water and then centrifuged for 10 min at 10000 rpm/min. Additionally, the crude product was dialyzed in ultrapure water by using a 1000 MWCO cellulose dialysis bag. Finally, solid U/D-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying and stored in the refrigerator at 4 ℃ for future characterization.

The QY of U/D-CDs was measured by the relative method. Quinine sulfate and rhodamine 6G were selected as the reference standard materials for the blue peak and the yellow-green peak respectively. QY of U/D-CDs was calculated as the following formula:
Φ x
=
Φ
s t
K x
/
K
s t
η x
/
η
s t
2
where Φ represents the FL quantum yield, K represents the slope of the linear fitting line, and η means the solvent's refractive index. Subscripts x and st represent U/D-CDs and reference standard materials, respectively.
To detect pH, the prepared U/D-CDs were dispersed into BR buffer solutions with different pH values. After 20 min of incubation, their FL emission spectra were recorded.

For detection of Cu2+, Cu2+ standard solution with different volumes was added into the mixture of BR buffer solution (pH = 5.4) containing 50 μL U/D-CDs, and then the obtained mixture was diluted to 1 mL with ultrapure water. After incubation at room temperature for 40 min, the FL emission spectra of the solution were recorded at excitation wavelengths of 300 nm and 680 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the determination of Cu2+ in real samples, tap water (laboratory) and river water (Beibei, Chongqing) samples were first collected and then filtered through the microporous membrane (0.22 μm). 50 μL U/D-CDs and Cu2+ standard solution with different volumes were added to the filtered water samples. After incubation for 40 min, the up/down-conversion FL emission spectra were recorded and measured five times in parallel to reduce the experimental error.

The morphology of U/D-CDs was observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a and 1b, U/D-CDs were well dispersed. The diameters of U/D-CDs were between 7.0 and 19.0 nm, with an average diameter of 13.20 nm. At the same time, the HRTEM image (the illustration in Fig. 1a) showed U/D-CDs have well-resolved lattice stripes with a lattice distance of 0.31 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphite [29]. The XRD pattern of U/D-CDs (Fig. 1c) showed a wide diffraction peak at around 25.0°, which corresponds to the graphitic plane (002), indicating that U/D-CDs have a typical amorphous carbon structure [28]. The surface functional groups of U/D-CDs were investigated by FT-IR spectra. As can be seen from Fig. 1d, the wide absorption peak centered at 3427 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H bonds, and the peak at 2978 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C–H. The absorption peaks at 1719, 1564, and 1461 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibration of C=O and C=C, respectively, and the peak at 1627 cm−1 proved the existence of amide bonds [30,31]. The absorption peak at 1342 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of C-N, and the peak at 1021 cm−1 was derived from the stretching vibration of C-O [32]. Moreover, the elemental composition of U/D-CDs was further studied by XPS. As shown in Fig. S1a, the three peaks at 284.8, 400.05, and 532.63 eV were related to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively, and the element contents were 78.68 %, 5.77 % and 15.54 %, respectively. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. S1b) was divided into three peaks at 284.4, 285.9, and 288.4 eV, which were attributed to C=C/C–C, C-O/C-N and –COOH, respectively [33]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. S1c) showed two peaks at 398.2 and 399.8 eV, which were related to C-N and N–H groups, respectively [34]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. S1d) can be divided into three peaks at 530.9, 531.6, and 532.9 eV that revealed the existence of C-O, C=O, and C-OH/C-O-C groups [35,36]. These results demonstrated that the prepared U/D-CDs were amorphous nanoparticles with –OH, –COOH, and –NH2 surface functional groups.

The optical properties of U/D-CDs were studied by UV–Vis absorption spectra and FL spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the strong absorption peak at 210 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of C=C, and the wide absorption peak at 248–275 nm was correlated with the n-π* transition of C=O [37]. In addition, the wide absorption peak at about 510 nm may be assigned to the n-π* transition of the surface functional group (C=O) of U/D-CDs, which is related to the surface state of U/D-CDs [38]. The FL emission spectra show that U/D-CDs have two different emission peaks at 421 nm and 544 nm, respectively. In addition, the U/D-CDs solution emitted strong yellow-green FL under 310 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 2a). This phenomenon was also confirmed by the Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates (0.276, 0.434) (Fig. 2b). The PL spectra of U/D-CDs solutions with different excitation wavelengths were further collected, as shown in Fig. 2c. Obviously, when the excitation wavelength increases from 250 to 350 nm, the emission peak position at 421 nm moves slightly and the emission peak at 544 nm exhibits excitation-independent property. Interestingly, the U/D-CDs solution also exhibits a unique up-conversion dual-emission behavior with the excitation wavelengths ranging from 640 to 740 nm (Fig. 2d). Moreover, the quantum yield (QY) of U/D-CDs at 421 nm and 544 nm are about 2.34 % and 17.45 %, respectively (Fig. S2). In addition, the effects of UV and salt solution on the up/down-conversion FL of U/D-CDs were also investigated (Fig. S3). The results show that U/D-CDs have good photobleaching resistance and salt resistance in the up/down-conversion dual-excitation channel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Up/down-conversion dual-channel ratiometric FL detection of pH
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the optical properties of U/D-CDs in BR buffer with different pH values were studied. To explore the optimal conditions for pH sensing, optimizing experiments were conducted. As shown in Fig. S4a, due to the rapid recognition of pH changes by the sensor, the sensor responds instantly when BR buffer solution (pH = 5) is added to U/D-CDs. Within 0–20 min, F 544/F 421 gradually increases, and F 544/F 421 gradually stabilizes after 20 min. Therefore, 20 min is chosen as the optimal incubation time. In addition, as the temperature increases, F 544/F 421 gradually increases (Fig. S4b). Considering the fast operation and simplicity of the experiment, 25 ℃ was chosen as the optimal experimental temperature. The FL emission spectra of U/D-CDs at different pH values were measured in the down-conversion excitation channel (λex = 300 nm). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the FL intensity of U/D-CDs at 544 nm increases sharply with pH increasing from 2.0 to 8.0, while the FL intensity of the emission band at 421 nm remains unchanged. Meanwhile, in the pH range of 2.0–8.0, the response of U/D-CDs to pH can be well fitted in the Boltzmann curve (Fig. 3b), with the equation of F 544/F 421 = 2.73–2.58/(1 + exp((pH-4.20)/0.62)) and the correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9998. Under 310 nm UV light, an obvious FL color change of the U/D-CDs solution from blue to yellow-green can be easily recognized with a gradual increase of pH (inset of Fig. 3a). In addition, the up-conversion excitation channel of U/D-CDs were also studied. As shown in Fig. S5a, the response of U/D-CDs to pH exhibited similar results to the down-conversion at 680 nm excitation wavelength. The FL intensity at 544 nm rises gradually with pH increasing from 2.0 to 8.0, while the emission peak at 432 nm is insensitive to pH. Moreover, the relationship between the ratio of intensity (F 544/F 432) and pH can also be fitted using the Boltzmann curve (Fig. S5b), with the equation of F 544/F 432 = 3.74–3.51/(1 + exp((pH-4.13)/0.60)) and a correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9999.

To explore the mechanism of pH response, the UV–vis absorption spectra of U/D-CDs solutions at different pH were measured. As shown in Fig. S6a, when the pH value decreases from 8.0 to 2.0, the absorption peak intensity of U/D-CDs at 510 nm and 248 nm gradually decreases. This result is related to the complex surface states of U/D-CDs. As the pH value increases, the carboxyl groups on the surface of U/D-CDs undergo deprotonation, which may lead to electrostatic doping/charging of U/D-CDs and change the Fermi level, producing new surface states with lower bandgaps, thereby facilitating the FL emission of U/D-CDs [9,39]. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. S6b, the zeta potential of U/D-CDs gradually decreases from −5.16 mV to −26.67 mV with the increase of pH, which corresponds to the protonation-deprotonation process of carboxyl groups of U/D-CDs.

To investigate the reliability of pH determination in complex samples, the selectivity and reversibility of the pH sensor were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3c and S5c, the ratios of FL intensity of U/D-CDs under the up/down-conversion dual-excitation channels were hardly affected in BR buffer solutions of different pH (3.0 and 6.0) when other ions and molecules were added, indicating that the pH sensor has excellent selectivity. Furthermore, the FL reversibility of U/D-CDs towards pH was studied (Fig. 3d and S5d). The pH was adjusted between 7.0 and 3.0 by adding HCl or NaOH to the U/D-CDs solution, and the ratio of FL intensity remained stable after five cycles, suggesting that the ratiometric FL sensor is reversible in up/down-conversion dual-excitation channels. Therefore, the ratiometric FL sensor achieved synchronous detection of pH under up/down-conversion dual-excitation channels, greatly improving the reliability of pH determination in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Up/down-conversion dual-channel ratiometric FL detection of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para:
Cu2+ can effectively quench the FL intensity of U/D-CDs, indicating the prepared CDs can be developed as an up/down-conversion dual-channel ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Cu2+ sensing. To achieve better analytical performance, detection parameters such as pH, buffer type, incubation time, and temperature were optimized. As shown in Fig. S7, the optimal pH value, buffer type, and incubation time are 5.4, BR buffer solution, and 40 min, respectively. In addition, with the increase of temperature, (F 544/F 421)0/(F 544/F 421) showed a decreasing trend. However, considering the simplicity of the experimental operation, 25 ℃ was chosen as the experimental temperature. Under the optimal detection conditions, the FL emission spectra of U/D-CDs solutions with different concentrations of Cu2+ were measured. As shown in Fig. 4 a, in the down-conversion excitation channel (λex = 300 nm), with the addition of different concentrations of Cu2+, the FL intensity of the emission peak at 544 nm significantly decreases, and the FL intensity at 421 nm slightly decreases. Meanwhile, the ratio of FL intensity (F 544/F 421) of U/D-CDs is linearly correlated with Cu2+ concentration in the ranges of 2–20 μM and 30–80 μM (Fig. 4b and Fig. S8a). The linear equations can be described as F 544/F 421 = -0.0162[Cu2+] + 2.4556 (R 2 = 0.9915) and F 544/F 421 = -0.0214[Cu2+] + 2.1702 (R 2 = 0.9921), respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) is 0.28 μM (
3 σ / k
, where σ is the relative standard deviation of the blank). In addition, it can be seen that the FL color of the U/D-CDs solution gradually changes from yellow-green to blue with the addition of Cu2+ under 310 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 4a), increasing the reliability and sensitivity.

In the up-conversion excitation channel (λex = 680 nm), the response of U/D-CDs to Cu2+ is similar to down-conversion (Fig. 4d). Moreover, there are also two excellent linear relationships between the ratio of FL intensity (F 544/F 432) and Cu2+ concentration in the ranges of 2–20 μM and 30–80 μM (Fig. 4e and Fig. S8b). The linear equations are F 544/F432 = -0.0182[Cu2+] + 3.3564 (R 2 = 0.9948) and F 544/F 432 = -0.0325[Cu2+] + 3.2499 (R 2 = 0.9942), respectively, and LOD is 0.32 μM. Compared with other fluorescent sensors for detecting Cu2+ (Table. S1), the ratiometric FL sensor has a wider linear range and an acceptable LOD. In addition, the strategy of up/down-conversion dual-channel synchronous detection of Cu2+ based on U/D-CDs has advantages, such as simple preparation and increased reliability in complex samples.

In order to explore the potential of the sensor for detecting Cu2+ in actual water samples, the effects of other different metal ions on the sensor were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4c and 4f, both in the down-conversion excitation channel (λex = 300 nm) and up-conversion excitation channel (λex = 680 nm), some metal ions including K+, Zn2+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Mo2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ce4+, Ni2+, and Cd2+ cannot cause obvious FL change of U/D-CDs. Meanwhile, there is no significant color change after adding other metal ions (Fig. S9). In addition, the FL quenching degree of U/D-CDs caused by the addition of different metal ions to U/D-CDs-Cu2+ is almost the same as the quenching degree caused by pure Cu2+ (Fig. S10). In addition, the influence of other common anions (Cl-, SO4 2-, CO3 2–, H2PO4 -, F-, ClO-, MnO4 2-, BrO3 -, and NO3 –) in water samples on the sensing of Cu2+ by U/D-CDs was also studied. As shown in Fig. S11a, the influence of other different anions on the FL of U/D-CDs was small or negligible. Meanwhile, when these anions were added to the Cu2+-U/D-CDs sensing system, it was observed that their effect on quenching FL was relatively small (Fig. S11b). Therefore, these results indicate that the ratiometric FL sensor is highly selective and has a good capacity for resistant interference. In addition, the up-conversion spectra of U/D-CDs were measured in the presence of fluorescent interfering substances (Fig. S12). The fluorescent interfering substance has down-conversion FL with no up-conversion. Meanwhile, the up-conversion spectrum of U/D-CDs is almost unchanged in the presence of interfering substances, further revealing that the up-conversion channel of U/D-CDs can eliminate the background interference effect of external interfering substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The FL quenching mechanism of U/D-CDs induced by Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the quenching mechanism of the Cu2+ sensor, the UV–Vis absorption spectra were studied. As shown in Fig. 5 a and 5b, the absorption spectra of U/D-CDs were significantly affected by the addition of Cu2+ ions. With the increase of the concentration of Cu2+, the absorption peak intensity at 510 nm gradually decreases, and a new absorption peak at 540 nm is formed, indicating a strong interaction between U/D-CDs and Cu2+, generating non-fluorescent ground state complexes [37,40]. Therefore, we proposed that the quenching process was attributed to a static quenching effect. To further demonstrate this mechanism, the FL lifetime decay curves of U/D-CDs were measured in the absence and presence of Cu2+. As shown in Fig. 5c and Table S2, the FL lifetimes of U/D-CDs before and after the addition of Cu2+ are 2.27 ns and 2.25 ns, respectively, with almost no change. Meanwhile, the zeta potential of U/D-CDs gradually increased from −16.0 mV to −5.20 mV with the continuous addition of Cu2+ (Fig. 5d), indicating that the surface functional groups of U/D-CDs interacted with Cu2+, consuming a large number of negative charges [24]. These results further confirm that the quenching mechanism of U/D-CDs can be attributed to the static quenching process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To study its significance and application in actual water samples, the FL sensor was used to determine Cu2+ in different real water samples (tap water and river water). Firstly, the collected water samples were filtered through the microporous membrane of 0.22 μm. Moreover, a certain amount of Cu2+ ions were added to the real water sample containing U/D-CDs using the standard addition method. After incubation for 40 min, their up/down-conversion FL spectra were recorded and measured three times in parallel. As shown in Table S3, in the down-conversion excitation channel (λex = 300 nm), the recoveries of Cu2+ in tap water and river water are between 90.53 % and 105.68 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are below 5.96 %. In the up-conversion excitation channel (λex = 680 nm), the recoveries of Cu2+ in tap water and river water are between 98.58 % and 113.58 % with small relative standard deviations (RSDs). Therefore, these results indicate the reliability of the ratiometric FL sensor in the determination of Cu2+.

In summary, a kind of novel up/down-conversion dual-emission carbon dots (U/D-CDs) were developed. The prepared U/D-CDs have good resistance to photobleaching and salt, exhibiting both up/down-conversion FL and dual-emission behavior. Based on the U/D-CDs, two probes were developed for pH and Cu2+ sensing, respectively, achieving sensitive dual-channel detection. Moreover, the rich FL color changes achieved the visualization analysis of pH and Cu2+. The developed ratiometric FL probe has been successfully used to analyze Cu2+ in tap water and river water. The developed probes improve their selectivity and sensitivity, and eliminate the background signal, which makes the probes suitable for complex sample analysis.

Yulin Zhu: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mengqi Zhu: Investigation, Data curation. Qiuju Zhou: Supervision, Methodology. Qunwei Shu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Kejun Tan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the research fund of the Chongqing College Students' Innovative Entrepreneurial Training Plan Program (No. S202310635085).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124648.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemically doped fluorescent carbon and graphene quantum dots for bioimaging, sensor, catalytic and photoelectronic applications
